content.p: As TerraModulus will construct a brand-new architecture to replace the old codebase structure from Minicraft+, all the characteristics should be renewed to meet the modern standards. Notably, the new codebase will be using Kotlin as its base language along with several subprojects and even side projects for enhanced structural plans of this project.

For the most cases, it is good to keep updated with the latest long-time-support (LTS) versions. If the major language level change is too frequent, it may cause certain maintenance drawbacks. Keeping updated with the latest could make sure the codebase is still meeting with the modern standards while solving stale issues. Since this project is for end product, there is no real need to keep the project language version as old as possible.

At the moment, Kotlin has fewer breaking changes than Java Development Kits (JDK), so it would be enough to keep up with Kotlin's latest version, while ensuring latest stable language features are available for project usages. However, for distributions, it is still necessary to release as the latest Java Virtual Machine (JVM) version for security updates and also JVM server features. This part could be aided by having the Launcher project for TerraModulus.

For Rust, the latest stable versions could be used without concerns, since new language features are still rolling out. This includes several experimental features that could aid development and quality assurance. However, new editions should be adapted only when they go stabled to ensure the minimal chances of language vulnerabilities.

As demonstrated in the above diagram, the TerraModulus project consists of several elements. It is worth noting that, the scale of this project may not be small; if possible, this project could greatly grow. For the convenience of references, several components in the aforementioned Architecture have been named with various code names.

On the Platform side, the Website Portal would allow client interactions and also resource management. As an abstract, several elements have to be drafted out to outline this Architecture better, but in general, there would be the Account System named Archora and the game itself named Orryx, along with user-created content hosted on a marketplace. The Mortared Launcher would act as a simple entry layer of an account over the entire platform system named Aetheron, and allow launching the game client instances.

For the game instances Orryx, there would be the client instance as Orryx Client and dedicated server instance as Orryx Server, where the client instances also include basic local hosting functionalities. Basically, Orryx Server would not have a client user interface but with commands or terminal controls for extensive hosting functions. In the codebase, Kotlin handles the abstraction logic of the game application, as Kryon, and Rust handles the low level game engine, as Ferricia. Kryon includes the user interface components, including but not limited to graphical and audio elements, as well as world objects; the game engine includes the rendering system, physics logics, networking and raw resource management, as well as other computational heavy tasks. Primora as the common core of Orryx would be referred to be shared components across Orryx Client and Orryx Server.

Other than those implementation entities, there is also the Execution Framework Proposal (EFP) system for standardization and general documentation under the TerraModulus community. The EFPs undergo their unique process alongside the implementation parts of the project. The management parts also include the community individuals, interacting with the entire community. [EFP 1]

In this architecture, there are certain roles for Orryx. Since the game instances may be offline, Archora and Aetheron may not be connected during the sessions on Orryx, but they are likely always connected when connecting to other server instances of Orryx over Ethernet or the Internet. Thus, authentication and verification of accounts shall only be done when connecting to any server. Version and instance management of Orryx shall only be done in the Mortared Launcher instead of in the instances of Orryx, except for the assets for an instance of Orryx.

The Internet conection in an instance of Orryx may not be required during a session. However, when the features requiring Internet connections are in use, the online status should be constantly checked to ensure consistency and data integrity. This keeps data synchronization and checks over session timeouts, especially from a server's perspective.

In order to ensure unified management of metadata and configurations, a single standard place for management should be done. Metadata of a Build should be generated during building of Orryx binaries, with the entries saved as a file entry named version.metadata in built binaries. Also, a Build Profile as a project file named build.profile shall be available in the repository root for the use of generation of certain Metadata.

Primary Metadata shall be directed by the Build Profile, for values of versions and stability status (as an entry named stable). It is not trivial to determine stability status by version strings, while development builds may still use version strings of stable versions. The primary version of Orryx would be saved as an entry named id, purely as an identifier without being analyzed in code, due to the complexity of the version strings and the uselessness of the value. Logics using properties of versioning rely on other version values, like the world format version.

There would be several generated Metadata, for build time (as an entry named build_time) and series name (as an entry named series). The build time would be the timestamp when this Metadata is being built while the series name would be directly the branch name of the Version Control System (VCS).
In code, there would be a class or struct representing the Metadata loaded during initialization, with all the values read-only. Such may be available in both Kryon and Ferricia.

The Build Profile would be in the format of Simple Plain Key-Value Entries without any additional features, with the constraints that: entry keys must only consist of lower Latin letters, underscores and periods in the ASCII table; there are two data types: string and boolean, depending on exact keys, while boolean values must be either true or false.
In Orryx, there are several versioning systems for different components of Orryx. Except for the Primary Version, all other versioning values should be comparable.

The Primary Version represents a release build of the entire Orryx in production environment. Since there may not be only one release series, while the main series would strictly follow the Compact Staged Versioning, others may not, so those version strings acting as identifiers may not always be comparable. However, those identifiers shall always and be guaranteed to be unique and only refer to exactly one version of a release build.

Certain Application Public Interfaces (APIs) may utilize Semantic Versioning (SemVer) for extensive and comprehensive versioning controls, although more standardized versioning would be recommended for more manageability.
Other versioning systems should be introduced when their components are being introduced.
